reasoning: The 1-year Treasury yield saw a massive 23-basis-point drop on Friday, August 1, 2025, falling from 4.10% to 3.87%. Such sharp single-day drops are usually tied to major macroeconomic news (likely the July employment report). Historically, when the 1-year yield drops rapidly into the 3.80s (as it did in September 2024 dropping to 3.92, and April 2025 dropping to 3.86), it has tended to bounce back above those levels within a few weeks or months once the initial market panic subsides or the rate cut expectations are adjusted. The FRED statistical tool gives a 0.71 probability estimate for a bounce in the short term (by Aug 10). I set the initial probability at 0.63 for the first date to reflect this tendency for a near-term partial mean reversion. The probabilities then smoothly converge toward 0.5 (0.58, 0.54, and 0.52) for the later resolution dates, as longer-horizon uncertainty dominates and the ultimate path of the Fed policy rate becomes the primary driver rather than short-term market reactions.
